description: Grade 11 track — Functions, Exponentials, Logarithms, Advanced Trigonometry, Sequences, Combinatorics, Matrices, Conic Sections, and intro Limits.

lessons.subject: Functions
lessons.subtopic: Domain, range, and function notation
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can find the domain and range of any basic function and use f(x) notation correctly
lessons.formula_spoken: f maps X to Y; the domain is the set of all valid inputs and the range is the set of all actual outputs
lessons.narration_style: warm and example-driven
lessons.seo_title: Functions Domain and Range — Complete Grade 11 Lesson
lessons.thumbnail_angle: What IS a function? CLEAR explanation
lessons.real_world_hook: A vending machine is a perfect function: you press B3 and you always get exactly one item. Press the same button twice — same result. That predictability is the definition of a function.
lessons.concept_intuition: A function is a rule that takes every input and assigns it exactly one output. No input can point to two different outputs.
lessons.visual_hints: Draw an arrow diagram: inputs on left, outputs on right, arrows showing the mapping. Show a non-function (one input, two arrows). Plot f(x)=x^2 on a number line showing domain vs range.
lessons.common_mistake: Confusing domain restrictions — students forget to exclude values that make a denominator zero or put a negative under a square root.

lessons.subject: Functions
lessons.subtopic: Evaluating and simplifying f(g(x))
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can form and evaluate composite functions and understand the order of operations
lessons.formula_spoken: f composed with g of x equals f of g of x — plug g into f
lessons.narration_style: step-by-step, machine analogy
lessons.seo_title: Composite Functions f(g(x)) — Grade 11 Full Lesson
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Plug one function INTO another
lessons.real_world_hook: Imagine a car wash: first the soap machine cleans (function g), then the dryer finishes (function f). The final result depends on doing them in the right order.
lessons.concept_intuition: Composition chains two functions: the output of the inner function becomes the input of the outer one.
lessons.visual_hints: Draw two machines labeled g and f with pipes connecting them. Show input x → g(x) → f(g(x)). Highlight order matters: f∘g ≠ g∘f.
lessons.common_mistake: Reversing the order — (f∘g)(x) means apply g FIRST, then f. Students often do f first.

lessons.subject: Functions
lessons.subtopic: Finding and verifying inverse functions
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can find the inverse of a function algebraically and verify using composition
lessons.formula_spoken: f of f-inverse of x equals x — composing a function with its inverse always returns x
lessons.narration_style: revealing the undo concept
lessons.seo_title: Inverse Functions — How to Find and Verify f Inverse
lessons.thumbnail_angle: The function that UNDOES itself
lessons.real_world_hook: Your GPS converts latitude/longitude to a street address. The inverse converts the street address back to coordinates. One undoes the other perfectly.
lessons.concept_intuition: An inverse function reverses the mapping — if f takes 3 to 7, then f⁻¹ takes 7 back to 3. Swap every input and output.
lessons.visual_hints: Show an arrow diagram and then reverse all arrows. Plot f and f⁻¹ on same axes — they are reflections across y=x. Step-by-step algebra: swap x and y, solve for y.
lessons.common_mistake: Writing f⁻¹(x) as 1/f(x). The superscript −1 means inverse, NOT a reciprocal.

lessons.subject: Functions
lessons.subtopic: Algebraic test and graphical symmetry
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can classify a function as even, odd, or neither using algebra and identify the axis or point of symmetry
lessons.formula_spoken: Even functions satisfy f of negative x equals f of x; odd functions satisfy f of negative x equals negative f of x
lessons.narration_style: clear contrast between two categories
lessons.seo_title: Even and Odd Functions — Algebraic Test Explained
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Even vs Odd — the symmetry trick
lessons.real_world_hook: A butterfly's wings are symmetric about a vertical axis — that is like an even function. A rotational pinwheel looks the same after a 180° spin — that is like an odd function.
lessons.concept_intuition: Even functions are symmetric about the y-axis (mirror image). Odd functions are symmetric about the origin (rotate 180° and it looks the same).
lessons.visual_hints: Plot f(x)=x² (even, y-axis symmetry) and f(x)=x³ (odd, origin symmetry) side by side. Show the substitution test on the board.
lessons.common_mistake: Thinking even/odd refers to the exponent only. Test algebraically — f(x)=x²+1 is even but f(x)=x²+x is neither.

lessons.subject: Functions
lessons.subtopic: Shifts, reflections, and stretches of f(x)
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can graph any transformed function by identifying vertical/horizontal shifts, reflections, and stretches from the equation
lessons.prerequisite: Domain and range, basic function graphs
lessons.formula_spoken: g of x equals a times f of b times x minus h, plus k — where h shifts horizontally, k shifts vertically, a stretches vertically, and b stretches horizontally
lessons.narration_style: visual and comparative, building the graph step by step
lessons.seo_title: Transformations of Functions — Shifts Stretches Reflections
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Move any graph in 4 directions
lessons.real_world_hook: A phone camera applies brightness, zoom, and position transforms to every photo. Mathematicians apply the same four operations to functions: shift, scale, reflect, stretch.
lessons.concept_intuition: Every transformation has a rule: adding to x shifts left (counterintuitive!), adding outside shifts up, multiplying by a negative reflects, multiplying by a number stretches or compresses.
lessons.visual_hints: Start with y=x². Show each transform one at a time on the same axes: shift right 2, shift up 3, reflect over x-axis, stretch by 2. Animate each step.
lessons.common_mistake: Shifting direction for horizontal moves — y=f(x−2) shifts RIGHT (not left) because x must equal 2 to get back to the original input.

lessons.subject: Exponential Functions
lessons.subtopic: Graphs, properties, and growth vs decay
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can graph exponential functions, identify growth vs decay, and understand the base's role
lessons.formula_spoken: f of x equals a times b to the power x, where b is a positive base not equal to 1
lessons.narration_style: energetic, showing real growth examples
lessons.seo_title: Exponential Functions — Growth Decay Graphs Explained
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Why money DOUBLES — exponential secret
lessons.real_world_hook: If you fold a paper 42 times, it would reach the Moon. Exponential growth seems slow at first, then explodes. Compound interest, viral videos, and population growth all follow this exact pattern.
lessons.concept_intuition: When b>1 the function grows — each step multiplies by the same factor. When 0<b<1 it decays. The base b determines the multiplication rate.
lessons.visual_hints: Graph y=2^x and y=(½)^x on the same axes. Mark the y-intercept at (0,1) for both. Show the horizontal asymptote y=0. Compare to linear growth side-by-side.
lessons.common_mistake: Confusing y=2^x (exponential) with y=x^2 (power function). The variable is in the EXPONENT for exponential functions.

lessons.subject: Exponential Functions
lessons.subtopic: Definition of e, natural exponential function y=eˣ
lessons.lesson_goal: Student understands why e≈2.718 is special, its origin from compound interest, and can evaluate e^x
lessons.formula_spoken: e is the limit as n approaches infinity of 1 plus 1 over n, raised to the n — approximately 2.71828
lessons.narration_style: story-driven, building from compound interest
lessons.seo_title: What is e? The Natural Number Explained Simply
lessons.thumbnail_angle: The most important number in math
lessons.real_world_hook: If a bank offers 100% annual interest, after 1 year $1 becomes $2. But if they compound every second, you get $2.718... No matter how often they compound, you can never exceed e dollars. That limit IS e.
lessons.concept_intuition: e is the unique base where the exponential function is its own rate of change — it grows at exactly its own size. That makes it the natural base for calculus.
lessons.visual_hints: Show the compound interest formula with n=1,2,4,12,365,∞. Table showing convergence to e. Plot y=eˣ vs y=2^x vs y=3^x showing e^x is between them.
lessons.common_mistake: Treating e as a variable. It is a fixed constant ≈ 2.718, like π. Never write 'solve for e'.

lessons.subject: Logarithmic Functions
lessons.subtopic: Definition, notation, and connection to exponentials
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can convert between exponential and logarithmic form and evaluate basic logarithms
lessons.formula_spoken: log base b of x equals y is equivalent to b to the power y equals x
lessons.narration_style: connecting logs to their exponential inverse
lessons.seo_title: Logarithms Explained Simply — What is log base b of x
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Logs are just BACKWARDS exponents
lessons.real_world_hook: The Richter scale for earthquakes uses logarithms. A magnitude 7 quake is not twice as strong as a magnitude 6 — it is TEN times stronger. Logs compress enormous ranges into manageable numbers.
lessons.concept_intuition: A logarithm asks: what power do I raise the base to in order to get this number? log₂(8)=3 because 2³=8. Logs are the inverse of exponentials.
lessons.visual_hints: Draw two columns: exponential form | log form. Show the switch between them with arrows. Graph y=log₂(x) as the reflection of y=2^x across y=x.
lessons.common_mistake: Reading log₂(8) as 2×8 or 2+8. The subscript is the BASE, not a multiplier.

lessons.subject: Logarithmic Functions
lessons.subtopic: Product, quotient, and power rules for logs
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can expand and condense logarithmic expressions using all three log laws
lessons.key_formula: \log(AB)=\log A+\log B,\quad \log\tfrac{A}{B}=\log A-\log B,\quad \log A^n=n\log A
lessons.formula_spoken: log of A times B equals log A plus log B; log of A over B equals log A minus log B; log of A to the n equals n times log A
lessons.narration_style: connecting each rule to the corresponding exponent law
lessons.seo_title: Laws of Logarithms — Product Quotient Power Rules
lessons.thumbnail_angle: 3 log rules you MUST know
lessons.real_world_hook: Engineers use log laws to turn multiplication of massive numbers into simple addition. Before calculators, scientists used log tables and these exact three rules to multiply million-digit numbers.
lessons.concept_intuition: Each log law mirrors an exponent law. Multiplication inside the log becomes addition outside (just like adding exponents when multiplying powers).
lessons.visual_hints: Write each rule next to its exponent counterpart. Show side-by-side: b^m · b^n = b^(m+n) vs log(AB)=logA+logB.
lessons.common_mistake: Writing log(A+B) = log(A) + log(B). This is WRONG. The product rule is for multiplication inside, not addition.

lessons.subject: Logarithmic Functions
lessons.subtopic: Solving equations using log and exponential properties
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can solve both exponential and logarithmic equations by applying inverse operations correctly
lessons.key_formula: \text{If } a^x = b \text{ then } x = \log_a b = \frac{\ln b}{\ln a}
lessons.formula_spoken: If a to the x equals b, then x equals log base a of b, which equals ln b divided by ln a by the change of base formula
lessons.narration_style: systematic solver, clear step labels
lessons.seo_title: Solving Exponential and Log Equations — Step by Step
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Crack ANY exponential equation
lessons.real_world_hook: Carbon dating determines the age of fossils by solving an exponential equation. Scientists measure remaining Carbon-14 and solve 0.5^(t/5730)=fraction to find t — the age in years.
lessons.concept_intuition: To solve exponential equations, take the log of both sides. To solve log equations, exponentiate both sides. Always undo the outer operation first.
lessons.visual_hints: Show two columns: solving exponential (take log) vs solving logarithmic (raise to power). Clearly label each inverse step.
lessons.common_mistake: Dividing by log instead of using change-of-base: 2^x=7 does not give x = 7/log2 but rather x = log7/log2.

lessons.subject: Trigonometry
lessons.subtopic: Coordinates, radians, and all-angle trig values
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can read exact trig values for any standard angle from the unit circle and convert between degrees and radians
lessons.key_formula: (\cos\theta, \sin\theta) \text{ are the coordinates of any point on the unit circle}
lessons.formula_spoken: The coordinates of any point on the unit circle are cosine theta and sine theta
lessons.narration_style: visual tour of the circle, building intuition systematically
lessons.seo_title: Unit Circle Explained — All Angles Radians and Exact Values
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Memorize the unit circle FOREVER
lessons.real_world_hook: Every animation in a video game — from rotating a character to spinning a wheel — is computed using the unit circle. The coordinates (cos θ, sin θ) tell the engine exactly where each point sits after rotation.
lessons.concept_intuition: Place a circle of radius 1 at the origin. For any angle θ, drop a perpendicular — the x-coordinate is cos θ, the y-coordinate is sin θ. The circle stores every trig value at once.
lessons.visual_hints: Draw the unit circle. Mark the four quadrants. Label 30°, 45°, 60° in Q1 with coordinates. Mirror into other quadrants showing sign changes. Show radian equivalents on the outer ring.
lessons.common_mistake: Mixing up which coordinate is sine and which is cosine. Remember: x is cosine (left-right), y is sine (up-down).

lessons.subject: Trigonometry
lessons.subtopic: Deriving and applying the three Pythagorean trig identities
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can derive all three Pythagorean identities from the unit circle and use them to simplify trig expressions
lessons.prerequisite: Unit circle, Pythagorean theorem, trig ratios
lessons.formula_spoken: sine squared theta plus cosine squared theta equals one — the fundamental Pythagorean identity
lessons.narration_style: deriving from first principles, connecting to geometry
lessons.seo_title: Pythagorean Trig Identities — Proof and Applications
lessons.thumbnail_angle: The identity that proves EVERYTHING
lessons.real_world_hook: Engineers simplifying signal equations use trig identities constantly to reduce complex expressions. Without these identities, physics formulas for waves and circuits would be unsolvable.
lessons.concept_intuition: On the unit circle, any point (cos θ, sin θ) satisfies x²+y²=1 by Pythagoras. That single circle equation gives us sin²θ+cos²θ=1 instantly.
lessons.visual_hints: Draw unit circle, mark point (cos θ, sin θ), draw the right triangle inside. Label sides. Apply Pythagoras to get identity. Then algebraically derive tan² and cot² versions.
lessons.common_mistake: Using sin²θ+cos²θ=1 but forgetting the derived versions: 1+tan²θ=sec²θ and 1+cot²θ=csc²θ.

lessons.subject: Trigonometry
lessons.subtopic: Expanding sin(A±B) and cos(A±B)
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can apply sum and difference identities to find exact trig values and prove other identities
lessons.formula_spoken: sine of A plus or minus B equals sine A cosine B plus or minus cosine A sine B
lessons.narration_style: methodical, pattern recognition focus
lessons.seo_title: Sum and Difference Identities — sin cos A+B Formulas
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Find sin(75°) exactly — no calculator
lessons.real_world_hook: To compute the exact position of a satellite at 75° without a calculator, engineers use sin(45°+30°) — breaking an unknown angle into two known ones.
lessons.concept_intuition: These formulas let you compute trig values for angles not on the standard unit circle by decomposing them into simpler pieces.
lessons.visual_hints: Write both formulas side by side (sin and cos versions). Show how 75°=45°+30° then plug in known values. Circle the pattern: the signs match for sin, flip for cos.
lessons.common_mistake: Writing sin(A+B)=sinA+sinB. This is completely wrong — trig does not distribute over addition.

lessons.subject: Trigonometry
lessons.subtopic: sin(2θ), cos(2θ), and their applications
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can derive and apply double angle formulas to simplify expressions and solve equations
lessons.prerequisite: Sum and difference formulas, Pythagorean identities
lessons.formula_spoken: sine of two theta equals two sine theta cosine theta; cosine of two theta equals cosine squared theta minus sine squared theta
lessons.narration_style: deriving from sum formula, then applying
lessons.seo_title: Double Angle Formulas — sin 2theta cos 2theta Explained
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Derived in 30 seconds from one formula
lessons.real_world_hook: Guitar strings vibrate at double frequencies when lightly touched at the midpoint — the physics of harmonics is modelled exactly by sin(2θ)=2sinθcosθ.
lessons.concept_intuition: Double angle formulas are just the sum formulas with A=B=θ. They are not new rules — they are shortcuts from the ones you already know.
lessons.visual_hints: Derive sin(2θ) live: sin(θ+θ)=sinθcosθ+cosθsinθ=2sinθcosθ. Show three forms of cos(2θ) and when each is useful.
lessons.common_mistake: Confusing cos(2θ)=2cos²θ−1 vs 1−2sin²θ vs cos²θ−sin²θ. All three are equivalent — choose based on what terms appear in the problem.

lessons.subject: Trigonometry
lessons.subtopic: Shape, period, and key features of sin and cos waves
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can sketch y=sinx and y=cosx, label all key points, and explain period, amplitude, and intercepts
lessons.formula_spoken: y equals sine x has a period of two pi, amplitude of 1, and range from negative 1 to 1
lessons.narration_style: tracing the wave from the unit circle to the graph
lessons.seo_title: Graphs of Sin and Cos — Period Amplitude Key Points
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Where do waves COME from? Unit circle!
lessons.real_world_hook: Every sound you hear — music, speech, noise — is a combination of sine waves. Your phone's speaker creates sounds by rapidly vibrating in the exact sine wave shape you will graph today.
lessons.concept_intuition: As the angle goes around the unit circle, the y-coordinate (sine) traces a wave. At 0° it starts at 0, reaches 1 at 90°, back to 0 at 180°, down to −1 at 270°, and home at 360°.
lessons.visual_hints: Split screen: unit circle on left, graph on right. Trace the point on the circle and simultaneously plot it on the graph. Show key points: (0,0), (π/2,1), (π,0), (3π/2,−1), (2π,0).
lessons.common_mistake: Confusing where sine and cosine start: sine starts at 0 (middle), cosine starts at 1 (top). Remember C for Cosine, C for Crown (starts at the top).

worked_example: y=\sin x: \text{starts at 0, one full wave every } 2\pi
y=\cos x: \text{starts at 1, shifted left by }\frac{\pi}{2} \text{ from sine}

lessons.subject: Trigonometry
lessons.subtopic: Graphing y = A sin(Bx + C) + D
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can identify and apply amplitude, period, phase shift, and vertical shift to graph any transformed trig function
lessons.prerequisite: Graphs of sin and cos, function transformations
lessons.formula_spoken: For y equals A sine of Bx minus C plus D: amplitude is A, period is 2 pi over B, and phase shift is C over B
lessons.narration_style: building each parameter one at a time
lessons.seo_title: Amplitude Period Phase Shift — Graphing Trig Functions
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Graph any sine wave in 4 steps
lessons.real_world_hook: Sound engineers adjust amplitude (volume), period (pitch), and phase to produce stereo effects. This lesson is literally how audio software works.
lessons.concept_intuition: A stretches the wave taller, B squeezes or widens it, C slides it left or right, D lifts or lowers it. Each parameter changes exactly one feature.
lessons.visual_hints: Start with base y=sinx. Add each parameter one at a time, redrawing the graph after each. Label the new period, amplitude, and key points.
lessons.common_mistake: Phase shift formula: for y=sin(2x−π), the phase shift is π/2 (not π). Divide C by B, don't just read C.

lessons.subject: Trigonometry
lessons.subtopic: arcsin, arccos, arctan — definition, range, and evaluation
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can evaluate inverse trig functions and understand their restricted domains and ranges
lessons.prerequisite: Inverse functions, unit circle, trig graphs
lessons.formula_spoken: arcsin of x returns the angle whose sine is x, restricted to outputs between negative pi over 2 and pi over 2
lessons.narration_style: careful about restrictions, contrasting with regular trig
lessons.seo_title: Inverse Trig Functions — arcsin arccos arctan Explained
lessons.thumbnail_angle: What angle gives sin = 0.5?
lessons.real_world_hook: GPS receivers compute the elevation angle to a satellite using arctan. Given horizontal and vertical distances, arctan gives the angle — no protractor needed.
lessons.concept_intuition: Trig functions repeat and are not one-to-one. To create an inverse, we restrict the domain to one cycle. arcsin is the inverse of sin on [−π/2, π/2].
lessons.visual_hints: Graph y=sinx and highlight the restricted portion [−π/2,π/2]. Show its reflection across y=x to get y=arcsinx. Do the same for cos and tan.
lessons.common_mistake: Thinking arcsin(sin(x))=x always. It only works if x is in the restricted range [−π/2, π/2].

lessons.subject: Sequences and Series
lessons.subtopic: Common ratio, nth term formula
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can identify the common ratio in a geometric sequence and find any term using the nth term formula
lessons.formula_spoken: the nth term equals the first term times r raised to the power n minus 1
lessons.narration_style: pattern discovery, building the formula
lessons.seo_title: Geometric Sequences — nth Term Formula Explained
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Find the 20th term instantly
lessons.real_world_hook: Every time a ball bounces, it reaches 70% of its previous height. After 10 bounces, how high is it? That is a geometric sequence with r=0.7.
lessons.concept_intuition: In a geometric sequence you multiply by the same ratio each time. To get to the nth term, multiply the starting value by r exactly (n−1) times.
lessons.visual_hints: Show sequence 2, 6, 18, 54... Highlight each term being multiplied by 3. Derive a_n by writing out a₁×r×r×...×r (n−1 times).
lessons.common_mistake: Using r^n instead of r^(n-1). The first term needs zero multiplications, so the exponent starts at 0.

lessons.subject: Sequences and Series
lessons.subtopic: Sum of finite geometric series
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can find the sum of any finite geometric series using the formula
lessons.formula_spoken: S sub n equals a sub 1 times 1 minus r to the n, divided by 1 minus r
lessons.narration_style: deriving the formula from first principles
lessons.seo_title: Geometric Series Sum Formula — Full Derivation and Examples
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Add 20 terms in 5 seconds
lessons.real_world_hook: A loan payment plan: you owe $1000 and pay 10% monthly. The total paid is a geometric series. Banks use this exact formula to compute your total payment before you sign.
lessons.concept_intuition: The derivation trick: write S, then write rS (each term shifted), subtract to cancel all middle terms, leaving only the first and last. This gives the compact formula.
lessons.visual_hints: Write S = a + ar + ar² + ... + arⁿ⁻¹. Below it write rS = ar + ar² + ... + arⁿ. Subtract to show S − rS = a − arⁿ. Factor and divide.
lessons.common_mistake: Applying the formula when |r|≥1 for an infinite series. Finite sum formula works for any r≠1; infinite sum only works for |r|<1.

lessons.subject: Sequences and Series
lessons.subtopic: Convergence condition and sum to infinity
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can determine if an infinite geometric series converges and find its exact sum
lessons.formula_spoken: S infinity equals a 1 over 1 minus r, valid only when the absolute value of r is less than 1
lessons.narration_style: building intuition through diminishing examples
lessons.seo_title: Infinite Geometric Series — Sum and Convergence Explained
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Add INFINITE terms and get a FINITE answer
lessons.real_world_hook: Zeno's paradox: to cross a room, first cross half, then half of the rest, then half again... infinitely. Yet you arrive. The sum 1/2+1/4+1/8+... = 1 exactly. Infinite terms, finite sum.
lessons.concept_intuition: When |r|<1, each term shrinks towards zero fast enough that the total stays bounded. As n→∞, the r^n term vanishes and S_∞ = a/(1−r).
lessons.visual_hints: Draw a number line from 0 to 1. Add 1/2, then 1/4, then 1/8... show it approaching 1 but never exceeding it. Then show the formula giving exactly 1.
lessons.common_mistake: Trying to use S_∞ when |r|≥1. The series diverges — the sum is infinite. Always check |r|<1 first.

lessons.subject: Sequences and Series
lessons.subtopic: Expanding (a+b)^n using Pascal's triangle and nCr
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can expand any binomial power using the Binomial Theorem and find specific terms
lessons.formula_spoken: a plus b to the n equals the sum from k equals 0 to n of n choose k times a to the n minus k times b to the k
lessons.narration_style: pattern-building through Pascal's triangle
lessons.seo_title: Binomial Theorem — Expand (a+b)^n with Pascal's Triangle
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Expand (x+2)^7 in 60 seconds
lessons.real_world_hook: Probability calculations for 'exactly k successes in n trials' use binomial coefficients directly. This theorem powers statistics, genetics, and computer science.
lessons.concept_intuition: Each term in the expansion comes from choosing which bracket contributes a b and which contributes an a. The coefficient n choose k counts those choices.
lessons.visual_hints: Show Pascal's triangle expanding row by row. Connect each row to (a+b)^0, (a+b)^1, (a+b)^2. Then show the general formula and label each part.
lessons.common_mistake: Forgetting the coefficient nCk. Students write aⁿ+bⁿ instead of the full expansion.

lessons.subject: Sequences and Series
lessons.subtopic: Reading and writing sums in sigma notation
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can read sigma notation, write series in sigma notation, and evaluate sums
lessons.formula_spoken: sigma from k equals 1 to n of a sub k means the sum of all terms from k equals 1 to k equals n
lessons.narration_style: decoding notation step by step
lessons.seo_title: Sigma Notation Explained — How to Read and Write Series
lessons.thumbnail_angle: What does Σ actually mean?
lessons.real_world_hook: Every programming loop that accumulates a total is executing a sigma sum. Understanding sigma notation means you already understand how computers sum data.
lessons.concept_intuition: Sigma (Σ) is shorthand for 'add all of these up'. The bottom says where to start, the top says where to stop, and the expression says what to add at each step.
lessons.visual_hints: Draw the sigma symbol and label each part: lower limit, upper limit, expression. Expand a sample sum by substituting k=1,2,3... one at a time.
lessons.common_mistake: Including k in the limit incorrectly — the index k runs through the specified values and is replaced by each value in the expression.

lessons.subject: Algebra
lessons.subtopic: Splitting rational expressions into simpler fractions
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can decompose a proper rational expression into partial fractions with linear and repeated factors
lessons.prerequisite: Polynomial long division, solving systems of equations
lessons.formula_spoken: P of x over the product of x minus a and x minus b equals A over x minus a plus B over x minus b
lessons.narration_style: systematic solver, covering the denominator trick
lessons.seo_title: Partial Fraction Decomposition — Step by Step Method
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Split any fraction into simple parts
lessons.real_world_hook: Engineers computing inverse Laplace transforms must split complex fractions. Every control system — your car's cruise control, a plane's autopilot — depends on this algebra technique.
lessons.concept_intuition: Partial fractions is the reverse of adding fractions: instead of finding a common denominator, you split a fraction BACK into simpler pieces.
lessons.visual_hints: Start with a combined fraction and show the forward direction (addition). Then reverse: set up the decomposition, multiply through, compare coefficients.
lessons.common_mistake: Forgetting to first check if the fraction is proper (degree of numerator < degree of denominator). If not, do polynomial long division first.

practice: \text{Find A and B using the cover-up method}

lessons.subject: Combinatorics
lessons.subtopic: Multiplication and addition principles for counting outcomes
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can use the multiplication principle to count the total number of outcomes in multi-stage events
lessons.formula_spoken: Total outcomes equals the product of the number of choices at each stage
lessons.narration_style: practical examples from everyday life
lessons.seo_title: Fundamental Counting Principle — Multiply Your Options
lessons.thumbnail_angle: How many outfits can you make?
lessons.real_world_hook: You have 4 shirts, 3 pants, and 2 shoes. If every combination is valid, how many outfits can you make? Multiply: 4×3×2=24. That is the Fundamental Counting Principle.
lessons.concept_intuition: If one event can happen m ways and another can happen n ways, and they are independent, then both together can happen m×n ways. Each stage multiplies the total.
lessons.visual_hints: Draw a tree diagram for a 3-choice, 2-choice scenario. Count all branches. Then show that multiplying gives the same answer faster.
lessons.common_mistake: Using addition instead of multiplication. Add only when events are mutually exclusive alternatives; multiply when all stages happen together.

lessons.subject: Combinatorics
lessons.subtopic: nPr formula — ordered arrangements without repetition
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can compute permutations using the nPr formula and recognise when order matters
lessons.formula_spoken: P of n comma r equals n factorial divided by the quantity n minus r factorial
lessons.narration_style: building from counting principle to formula
lessons.seo_title: Permutations nPr — When Order Matters in Counting
lessons.thumbnail_angle: ORDER matters — this is NOT combinations
lessons.real_world_hook: How many ways can 10 runners finish in 1st, 2nd, and 3rd place? The winner matters — it is not the same as picking three runners for a team. That is a permutation.
lessons.concept_intuition: A permutation is an ordered arrangement. For the first slot you have n choices, for the second n−1, etc. Multiply r of these falling factorials — that is nPr.
lessons.visual_hints: Show the slot method: 10_× 9_× 8_ for 3 positions from 10 people = 720. Then derive this matches 10!/(10-3)! = 10!/7!.
lessons.common_mistake: Using permutations when order does not matter. If you are picking a committee of 3 where no one has a special title, that is a combination, not a permutation.

lessons.subject: Combinatorics
lessons.subtopic: nCr formula — unordered selections
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can compute combinations using the nCr formula and distinguish combinations from permutations
lessons.formula_spoken: n choose r equals n factorial over r factorial times n minus r factorial
lessons.narration_style: contrasting with permutations, building the divide-by-r! intuition
lessons.seo_title: Combinations nCr — When Order Does NOT Matter
lessons.thumbnail_angle: The difference between nPr and nCr
lessons.real_world_hook: Choosing 5 players from a squad of 22 for a starting lineup where positions are fixed — that is combinations. It does not matter in which order you call their names.
lessons.concept_intuition: A combination is an unordered selection. We take the permutation count and divide by r! to cancel out all the orderings of the r chosen items.
lessons.visual_hints: Show {A,B,C} — list all 6 permutations, then group them into 1 combination. This shows why we divide by 3!=6. Extend to nCr formula.
lessons.common_mistake: Using nPr instead of nCr when order does not matter — this overcounts by a factor of r!

lessons.subject: Probability
lessons.subtopic: Sample space, events, and probability formula P(E)=n(E)/n(S)
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can list sample spaces, identify events, and compute classical probability using the ratio formula
lessons.formula_spoken: Probability of E equals the number of favourable outcomes divided by the total number of outcomes in the sample space
lessons.narration_style: systematic, using tangible examples throughout
lessons.seo_title: Classical Probability — Sample Space and P(E) Formula
lessons.thumbnail_angle: What are the REAL chances?
lessons.real_world_hook: Insurance companies compute the exact probability you will have an accident based on classical probability models. The premium you pay is a direct result of this formula.
lessons.concept_intuition: If all outcomes are equally likely, the probability of an event is simply how many outcomes you want divided by how many outcomes exist total.
lessons.visual_hints: List all outcomes of rolling two dice (36 total). Highlight outcomes summing to 7 (6 outcomes). Compute P(sum=7)=6/36=1/6.
lessons.common_mistake: Forgetting that classical probability requires equally likely outcomes. Drawing a card is equally likely; picking a friend to call is not (depends on who you are close to).

lessons.subject: Probability
lessons.subtopic: P(A|B) definition, Bayes' theorem intuition
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can compute conditional probability and apply the multiplication rule for dependent events
lessons.formula_spoken: The probability of A given B equals the probability of A intersect B divided by the probability of B
lessons.narration_style: building intuition through narrowing the sample space
lessons.seo_title: Conditional Probability P(A|B) — Bayes Theorem Intro
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Updated probability — Bayes' secret
lessons.real_world_hook: A medical test is 95% accurate. You test positive. What is the probability you actually have the disease? Surprisingly, it might be less than 50%. Conditional probability and Bayes' theorem reveal the truth.
lessons.concept_intuition: Conditional probability restricts the sample space. Given that B happened, what portion of B's outcomes also have A? That is P(A|B).
lessons.visual_hints: Draw a Venn diagram. Shade B. Ask: within the shaded region, what fraction is A∩B? That fraction is P(A|B).
lessons.common_mistake: Confusing P(A|B) with P(B|A). These are different — testing positive given disease ≠ having disease given positive test.

lessons.subject: Matrices
lessons.subtopic: Order, types, and elements of a matrix
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can identify matrix order, locate specific elements using row-column notation, and recognise square, row, column, and identity matrices
lessons.key_formula: A = [a_{ij}],\quad a_{ij} \text{ is in row } i, \text{ column } j
lessons.formula_spoken: Matrix A contains elements a sub ij, where i is the row number and j is the column number
lessons.narration_style: clear and methodical, grid-based visual explanation
lessons.seo_title: Introduction to Matrices — Order Types and Elements
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Grids of numbers that do EVERYTHING
lessons.real_world_hook: Netflix's recommendation system stores every user's ratings for every movie in a giant matrix. Each row is a user, each column is a movie. This is how machines learn your taste.
lessons.concept_intuition: A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers organised in rows and columns. The size is described as m×n (m rows, n columns).
lessons.visual_hints: Draw a 2×3 matrix with labeled rows and columns. Point to each element and state its position (a₁₁, a₁₂, etc.). Show identity matrix and zero matrix as special cases.
lessons.common_mistake: Confusing row and column order — it is always ROW × COLUMN (RC like a Remote Control). So a 3×2 matrix has 3 rows and 2 columns.

practice: \text{State the order and find } a_{22} \text{ for } \begin{pmatrix}7&8\\9&10\end{pmatrix}

lessons.subject: Matrices
lessons.subtopic: Element-wise addition and scalar scaling
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can add two matrices of the same order and multiply a matrix by a scalar
lessons.formula_spoken: Add matrices element by element; multiply each element by the scalar k
lessons.narration_style: clear grid-by-grid demonstration
lessons.seo_title: Matrix Addition and Scalar Multiplication Explained
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Adding grids of numbers — simple rules
lessons.real_world_hook: Adding two matrices is how economists combine data tables — one table for January sales, another for February, sum them for the semester total.
lessons.concept_intuition: Matrix addition is just pairwise: add each element in the same position. Scalar multiplication just scales every element by the same factor.
lessons.visual_hints: Show two 2×2 matrices side-by-side with arrows pointing to corresponding elements being added. Then show scalar multiplication doubling each element.
lessons.common_mistake: Trying to add matrices of different sizes — this is undefined. Both matrices must have the exact same order.

lessons.subject: Matrices
lessons.subtopic: Row-by-column dot product method
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can multiply two compatible matrices using the row-column dot product rule and verify dimensions
lessons.formula_spoken: The i-j element of AB is the dot product of the i-th row of A with the j-th column of B
lessons.narration_style: row-by-column, systematic and visual
lessons.seo_title: Matrix Multiplication — Row Column Method Step by Step
lessons.thumbnail_angle: NOT like regular multiplication — learn this!
lessons.real_world_hook: Every 3D video game transforms millions of points by multiplying matrices 60 times per second. Your GPU is a matrix multiplication machine.
lessons.concept_intuition: To get element (i,j) of the product, take row i of A and column j of B, multiply pairs, add. The resulting matrix has dimensions (rows of A) × (columns of B).
lessons.visual_hints: Highlight row 1 of A in red and column 1 of B in blue. Show the dot products. Build the full 2×2 result step by step. Then show a non-square example.
lessons.common_mistake: Assuming AB = BA. Matrix multiplication is NOT commutative in general.

lessons.subject: Matrices
lessons.subtopic: Formula, geometric meaning, and singular matrices
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can compute the determinant of a 2×2 matrix and interpret its geometric meaning
lessons.formula_spoken: The determinant of a 2 by 2 matrix equals a d minus b c
lessons.narration_style: formulaic then geometric interpretation
lessons.seo_title: Determinant of 2x2 Matrix — Formula and Geometric Meaning
lessons.thumbnail_angle: ad minus bc — what it really means
lessons.real_world_hook: A determinant of zero means the matrix transformation collapses a 2D region onto a line — it is singular and has no inverse. This is why det=0 appears in structural engineering failure analysis.
lessons.concept_intuition: The determinant measures the scale factor by which the matrix transformation stretches areas. If det=0, the matrix squashes the plane flat — no reverse is possible.
lessons.visual_hints: Cross-multiply visually on the matrix: diagonal ad goes one way (+), diagonal bc goes the other (−). Show a unit square being transformed and its area equaling |det|.
lessons.common_mistake: Subtracting in the wrong order — it is ad MINUS bc, not bc MINUS ad.

lessons.subject: Matrices
lessons.subtopic: Formula for inverse and conditions for existence
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can find the inverse of a 2×2 matrix using the formula and verify with matrix multiplication
lessons.formula_spoken: A inverse equals 1 over det A times the matrix with swapped diagonal and negated off-diagonal elements
lessons.narration_style: two clear steps: swap and negate, then divide by det
lessons.seo_title: Inverse of 2x2 Matrix — Formula and How to Verify
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Reverse ANY matrix transformation
lessons.real_world_hook: Cryptographers encode messages by multiplying by a key matrix. To decode, they multiply by the inverse matrix. The inverse undoes the encryption.
lessons.concept_intuition: The inverse of A is the matrix that undoes A. When you multiply A by A⁻¹ you get the identity matrix — the matrix equivalent of multiplying by 1.
lessons.visual_hints: Show the 4-step recipe: (1) find det, (2) swap a and d, (3) negate b and c, (4) divide all elements by det. Verify by showing AA⁻¹=I.
lessons.common_mistake: Forgetting to divide by the determinant — the swap and negate part alone does not give the inverse.

lessons.subject: Matrices
lessons.subtopic: Matrix form AX=B and Cramer's Rule for 2×2 systems
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can express a 2×2 linear system as AX=B and solve it using the inverse matrix or Cramer's Rule
lessons.prerequisite: Inverse of a matrix, determinants, systems of equations
lessons.formula_spoken: The solution vector X equals A inverse times B
lessons.narration_style: connecting algebra to matrix language
lessons.seo_title: Solving Systems of Equations Using Matrices — Cramer's Rule
lessons.thumbnail_angle: One matrix solves ANY linear system
lessons.real_world_hook: Air traffic control models the position of aircraft as systems of linear equations. Solving them in milliseconds using matrix methods is what makes simultaneous tracking possible.
lessons.concept_intuition: A system ax+by=e, cx+dy=f can be written as one matrix equation AX=B. Multiply both sides by A⁻¹ on the left to get X=A⁻¹B — the solution in one step.
lessons.visual_hints: Write the system, then pack it into AX=B form. Find A⁻¹, multiply by B. Also show Cramer's Rule: x=det(Aₓ)/det(A) as a visual alternative.
lessons.common_mistake: Left-multiplying incorrectly — matrix multiplication is not commutative, so X=A⁻¹B not BA⁻¹.

lessons.subject: Conic Sections
lessons.subtopic: Vertex form, focus, directrix, and axis of symmetry
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can write a parabola in vertex form, identify vertex, axis, focus, and directrix, and sketch the curve
lessons.formula_spoken: x minus h squared equals 4p times y minus k; the focus is at h, k plus p
lessons.narration_style: architectural, building the curve from its definition
lessons.seo_title: Parabolas — Vertex Focus Directrix Fully Explained
lessons.thumbnail_angle: The shape satellites and headlights share
lessons.real_world_hook: Satellite dishes and car headlights are parabolas. Any signal hitting the dish reflects to the exact focal point. This is not a coincidence — it is the mathematical definition of a parabola.
lessons.concept_intuition: A parabola is the set of all points equally far from the focus point and the directrix line. This equal-distance property creates the distinctive U-shape.
lessons.visual_hints: Draw focus and directrix. Show several points equidistant from each. Connect them to trace the parabola. Highlight vertex at midpoint between focus and directrix.
lessons.common_mistake: Confusing vertical vs horizontal parabolas. (x-h)²=4p(y-k) opens vertically; (y-k)²=4p(x-h) opens horizontally.

lessons.subject: Conic Sections
lessons.subtopic: Standard form, foci, vertices, and axes
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can write an ellipse in standard form, find foci, vertices, and axes, and sketch the curve
lessons.prerequisite: Circles, Pythagorean theorem, conic section definition
lessons.formula_spoken: x minus h squared over a squared plus y minus k squared over b squared equals 1; c squared equals a squared minus b squared
lessons.narration_style: geometric and spatial
lessons.seo_title: Ellipses — Standard Form Foci and Axes Explained
lessons.thumbnail_angle: The oval that rules planetary motion
lessons.real_world_hook: Every planet's orbit is an ellipse with the sun at one focus — Kepler's first law. Understanding ellipses is literally understanding how the solar system works.
lessons.concept_intuition: An ellipse is the set of all points where the sum of distances to two fixed foci is constant. The bigger axis is the major axis; the smaller is the minor axis.
lessons.visual_hints: Draw two foci. Show a string of fixed length stretched between them and a pencil tracing the ellipse. Label a, b, and c. Show c² = a² − b² in a right triangle inside the ellipse.
lessons.common_mistake: Placing foci on the shorter axis. Foci are always on the MAJOR (longer) axis.

lessons.subject: Conic Sections
lessons.subtopic: Standard form, foci, vertices, and asymptotes
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can write a hyperbola in standard form, identify foci, vertices, and asymptotes, and sketch both branches
lessons.formula_spoken: x squared over a squared minus y squared over b squared equals 1; c squared equals a squared PLUS b squared
lessons.narration_style: contrasting with ellipse, building from the subtraction
lessons.seo_title: Hyperbolas — Standard Form Foci Asymptotes Explained
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Two curves racing to infinity
lessons.real_world_hook: Radio navigation systems (LORAN) use hyperbolas to locate ships: the difference in arrival times of two signals from different towers traces a hyperbola — your position is on it.
lessons.concept_intuition: A hyperbola is where the DIFFERENCE of distances to two foci is constant (unlike an ellipse where it's the SUM). This creates two separate branches diverging towards asymptotes.
lessons.visual_hints: Show ellipse (sum=constant) vs hyperbola (difference=constant). Draw the two branches and both asymptotes y=±(b/a)x. Show the central box used to find asymptotes.
lessons.common_mistake: Using c²=a²−b² (ellipse formula) for a hyperbola. For hyperbolas c²=a²+b² because the foci are OUTSIDE the curve.

lessons.subject: Limits
lessons.subtopic: The intuitive idea of a limit — approaching a value
lessons.lesson_goal: Student understands what a limit means intuitively and can evaluate simple limits by substitution or inspection of a table
lessons.formula_spoken: the limit of f of x as x approaches a equals L — f gets arbitrarily close to L as x gets close to a
lessons.narration_style: story-driven, zooming in on the curve
lessons.seo_title: Introduction to Limits — What Does a Limit Mean?
lessons.thumbnail_angle: What happens when x gets VERY close?
lessons.real_world_hook: Speed cameras don't freeze time — they calculate your speed over a very short distance. As that distance shrinks toward zero, you get instantaneous speed. That is a limit.
lessons.concept_intuition: A limit asks: as the input gets closer and closer to a value (without touching it), what is the output approaching? The limit is the destination, not the current value.
lessons.visual_hints: Graph f(x)=(x²-1)/(x-1). Show the hole at x=1. Use a table of values approaching from left and right. Both sides approach 2, so the limit is 2 even though f(1) is undefined.
lessons.common_mistake: Thinking the limit equals the function value f(a). The limit is what f APPROACHES — f(a) might not even exist at that point.

lessons.subject: Limits
lessons.subtopic: Substitution, factoring, and rationalizing to evaluate limits
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can evaluate limits algebraically using direct substitution, factoring, or rationalisation when direct substitution fails
lessons.prerequisite: Introduction to limits, polynomial factoring, rationalisation
lessons.key_formula: \text{If direct sub gives }\tfrac{0}{0},\text{ factor and cancel, then substitute}
lessons.formula_spoken: If substitution gives 0 over 0, factor and cancel the offending term, then substitute
lessons.narration_style: three clear techniques, applied in sequence
lessons.seo_title: Evaluating Limits Algebraically — 3 Techniques Explained
lessons.thumbnail_angle: Fix 0/0 in 3 steps
lessons.real_world_hook: Physicists evaluating energy equations at critical temperatures face 0/0 forms constantly. Algebraic limit techniques — not calculators — give the correct physical answer.
lessons.concept_intuition: Direct substitution is always tried first. When it gives 0/0, the function has a removable discontinuity — factor to cancel the common term, then substitute.
lessons.visual_hints: Three-column board: Technique | When to use | Example. Row 1: substitution. Row 2: factor and cancel. Row 3: multiply by conjugate (for square roots).
lessons.common_mistake: Stopping at 0/0 and saying the limit does not exist. Always try to simplify first — 0/0 is indeterminate, not undefined.

lessons.subject: Limits
lessons.subtopic: Horizontal asymptotes and behaviour as x→±∞
lessons.lesson_goal: Student can evaluate limits at infinity for rational functions and connect them to horizontal asymptotes
lessons.prerequisite: Limits algebraically, rational functions, degree of polynomials
lessons.key_formula: \lim_{x\to\infty}\frac{p(x)}{q(x)}: \text{ compare degrees of numerator and denominator}
lessons.formula_spoken: For limits at infinity of rational functions, compare the degree of the numerator to the degree of the denominator
lessons.narration_style: three-case analysis, methodical and clear
lessons.seo_title: Limits at Infinity — Horizontal Asymptotes of Rational Functions
lessons.thumbnail_angle: What happens as x goes to INFINITY?
lessons.real_world_hook: Engineers designing filters check what happens to circuit output at very high frequencies — mathematically, that is a limit as x→∞. The horizontal asymptote is the long-run output.
lessons.concept_intuition: As x gets huge, lower-degree terms become negligible. Only the leading terms matter. Three cases: degree top < bottom (limit=0), equal (ratio of leading coefficients), top > bottom (limit=±∞).
lessons.visual_hints: Show all three cases on separate graphs. Label the horizontal asymptote on each. Demonstrate dividing numerator and denominator by x^n (highest power in denominator).
lessons.common_mistake: Applying the rule to non-rational functions. For polynomials the limit is always ±∞; only for rational functions do we get finite limits.
